Title: The chloroalkaloid (−)-acutumine is biosynthesized via a Fe(II)- and 2-oxoglutarate-dependent halogenase in Menispermaceae plants

Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: In vitro assays with Fe(II), 2-oxoglutarate, and NaCl
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Site-directed mutagenesis; recombinant expression and purification; in vitro enzyme assay; LC-HRAM-MS
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of halogenase activity and gain of predominant hydroxylase activity on (−)-dechloroacutumine, demonstrating mechanistic importance of Gly226
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Provides mechanistic basis for engineering reaction outcome between halogenation and hydroxylation in plant 2OG enzymes
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: This engineered mutant restored the canonical Asp in the facial triad and predominantly produced 11-hydroxy-dechloroacutumine, with chlorination abolished/undetectable as functional output of DAH
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. G226D substitution tests the unusual active-site residue unique to DAH. 2. The mutant loses halogenase activity and instead hydroxylates (−)-dechloroacutumine. 3. This validates that the Asp-to-Gly substitution is the key mechanistic change enabling plant halogenation chemistry.

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Terminal halogenation branch of acutumine pathway
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes 2-oxoglutarate-dependent chlorination of (−)-dechloroacutumine to form (−)-acutumine; also retains trace hydroxylation activity and can support azidation in the presence of azide
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Selective cytotoxicity to cultured human T cells; memory-enhancing activity in Wistar rat model
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Highly expressed in roots; 459-fold higher transcript level in roots than leaves in M. canadense
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Relatively enriched in roots, consistent with root accumulation of (−)-acutumine
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: In vitro assays required Fe(II), 2-oxoglutarate, NaCl; azidation observed with NaN3
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes biosynthesis of the chloroalkaloid (−)-acutumine by catalyzing the terminal chlorination step from (−)-dechloroacutumine
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Terminal determinant of chloroalkaloid formation; key committed chlorination step for final product identity
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for metabolic engineering of halogenated or azidated plant natural products and as a starting point for directed evolution to expand substrate and anion selectivity
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: RNA-seq: NCBI SRA SRR10947794-SRR10947810; de novo transcriptomes: NCBI TSA GIIU00000000.1, GIIT00000000.1, GIIS00000000.1
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative transcriptomics; tissue-specific expression analysis; recombinant protein expression in E. coli; in vitro enzyme assay with substrate/product LC-HRAM-MS; phylogenetic analysis
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Contains unusual Asp226Gly facial-triad substitution characteristic of 2ODHs; first plant-origin 2ODH reported; no DAH ortholog found in non-producer Stephania japonica
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. McDAH was identified from M. canadense root transcriptome as the strongest 2ODD-family candidate correlated with (−)-acutumine accumulation. 2. It encodes a plant Fe(II)/2-oxoglutarate-dependent halogenase with the distinctive Asp226Gly substitution. 3. DAH catalyzes the terminal chlorination of (−)-dechloroacutumine to produce (−)-acutumine, defining the last step of the pathway.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Sinomenium acutum; heterologous Escherichia coli expression system
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Terminal halogenation branch of acutumine pathway
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes 2-oxoglutarate-dependent chlorination of (−)-dechloroacutumine to yield (−)-acutumine; also forms trace 11-hydroxy-dechloroacutumine and catalyzes azidation to 11-azidodechloroacutumine in the presence of azide
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Selective cytotoxicity to cultured human T cells; memory-enhancing activity in Wistar rat model
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Identified as McDAH ortholog in S. acutum root transcriptome; 99.1% protein identity to McDAH
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: In vitro chlorination required Fe(II), 2-oxoglutarate, NaCl; azidation observed with NaN3; no activity detected on several non-native alkaloids
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Directly converts (−)-dechloroacutumine to (−)-acutumine and can also derivatize the substrate by azidation under azide supplementation
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Terminal determinant of final chlorinated product identity
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Promising biocatalyst for derivatization of plant natural products, including halogenation and azidation; candidate for directed evolution and metabolic engineering
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: RNA-seq: NCBI SRA SRR10947794-SRR10947810; de novo transcriptomes: NCBI TSA GIIU00000000.1, GIIT00000000.1, GIIS00000000.1
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Recombinant protein purification from E. coli; in vitro enzyme assays; LC-HRAM-MS metabolite profiling; kinetic assays; alternative substrate/anion testing
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Km 18.35 ± 7.13 μM and apparent kcat 63.44 ± 8.75 min−1 toward (−)-dechloroacutumine; highly substrate-specific; first biochemically characterized plant 2ODH
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SaDAH is an ortholog of McDAH sharing 99.1% amino-acid identity. 2. Purified recombinant SaDAH chlorinates (−)-dechloroacutumine to form (−)-acutumine in a 2OG-dependent reaction. 3. SaDAH also shows low hydroxylation and azidation promiscuity, expanding its potential utility as a biocatalyst.
